title: icomb
text: Forecast reconciliation used make forecasts coherent across hierarchy grouped structure. Australian tourism data, trips can aggregated State, Purpose, combinations. forecasts produced independently series, results generally add correctly across structure. vignette demonstrates workflow goes step reconciliation alone. Alongside coherent forecasts, also examine series selected icomb, whether series exhibit distinctive time series characteristics. analysis proceeds four stages: construct grouped tourism structure fit base forecast models reconcile forecasts extract series inclusion information icomb relate inclusion series feature-based summaries accuracy series.

title: icomb
code: library(dplyr) library(tsibble) library(fable) library(icomb) library(feasts) library(ggplot2) library(plotly)

title: icomb
text: begin tourism dataset available tsibble package aggregate trips across State Purpose dimensions create simple grouped structure. grouped structure contains total, marginal aggregates (.e., state level aggregates purpose level aggregates), bottom-level series defined State Purpose combination. connected series form natural setting forecast reconciliation.
code: tourism_gts <- tourism |>    aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 Trips = sum(Trips)) tourism_gts #> # A tsibble: 3,600 x 4 [1Q] #> # Key:       State, Purpose [45] #> Loading required namespace: crayon #>    Quarter State        Purpose       Trips #>      <qtr> <chr*>       <chr*>        <dbl> #>  1 1998 Q1 <aggregated> <aggregated> 23182. #>  2 1998 Q2 <aggregated> <aggregated> 20323. #>  3 1998 Q3 <aggregated> <aggregated> 19827. #>  4 1998 Q4 <aggregated> <aggregated> 20830. #>  5 1999 Q1 <aggregated> <aggregated> 22087. #>  6 1999 Q2 <aggregated> <aggregated> 21458. #>  7 1999 Q3 <aggregated> <aggregated> 19914. #>  8 1999 Q4 <aggregated> <aggregated> 20028. #>  9 2000 Q1 <aggregated> <aggregated> 22339. #> 10 2000 Q2 <aggregated> <aggregated> 19941. #> # ℹ 3,590 more rows

title: icomb
text: next fit ETS model every series grouped structure. can fit univariate models multivariate model(s). base forecasts. Since series modelled independently, forecasts guaranteed coherent aggregation constraints.
code: fit <- tourism_gts |>    model(base = ETS(Trips)) fit #> # A mable: 45 x 3 #> # Key:     State, Purpose [45] #>    State           Purpose              base #>    <chr*>          <chr*>            <model> #>  1 ACT             Business     <ETS(M,N,M)> #>  2 ACT             Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  3 ACT             Other        <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  4 ACT             Visiting     <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  5 ACT             <aggregated> <ETS(M,A,N)> #>  6 New South Wales Business     <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  7 New South Wales Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  8 New South Wales Other        <ETS(A,N,N)> #>  9 New South Wales Visiting     <ETS(A,N,A)> #> 10 New South Wales <aggregated> <ETS(A,N,A)> #> # ℹ 35 more rows

title: icomb
text: reconcile base forecasts using two methods. ols: MinT reconciliation \mathbf{W}_h \propto \mathbf{} icomb: icomb reconciliation method group lasso penalty (default option) icomb method group lasso penalty especially useful exploratory analysis allows us study series included information combination process.
code: fit_recon <- fit |>    reconcile(     ols = min_trace(base, method = "ols"),     icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 55)   ) fit_recon #> # A mable: 45 x 5 #> # Key:     State, Purpose [45] #>    State           Purpose              base ols          icomb        #>    <chr*>          <chr*>            <model> <model>      <model>      #>  1 ACT             Business     <ETS(M,N,M)> <ETS(M,N,M)> <ETS(M,N,M)> #>  2 ACT             Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  3 ACT             Other        <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  4 ACT             Visiting     <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  5 ACT             <aggregated> <ETS(M,A,N)> <ETS(M,A,N)> <ETS(M,A,N)> #>  6 New South Wales Business     <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  7 New South Wales Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  8 New South Wales Other        <ETS(A,N,N)> <ETS(A,N,N)> <ETS(A,N,N)> #>  9 New South Wales Visiting     <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> #> 10 New South Wales <aggregated> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> #> # ℹ 35 more rows

title: icomb
text: investigate behaviour icomb, glance reconciliation results provides .included variable indicating series selected icomb reconciliation.
code: glance_output <- fit_recon |>    glance() glance_output #> # A tibble: 135 × 12 #>    State  Purpose  .model sigma2 log_lik   AIC  AICc   BIC   MSE  AMSE #>    <chr*> <chr*>   <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 ACT    Business base   0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  2 ACT    Business ols    0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  3 ACT    Business icomb  0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  4 ACT    Holiday  base   0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  5 ACT    Holiday  ols    0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  6 ACT    Holiday  icomb  0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  7 ACT    Other    base   0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #>  8 ACT    Other    ols    0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #>  9 ACT    Other    icomb  0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #> 10 ACT    Visiting base   0.0305   -450.  905.  905.  912.  965. 1038. #> # ℹ 125 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: MAE <dbl>, .included <lgl>

title: icomb
text: describe series structure statistically, compute broad collection features using feasts. features capture properties trend strength, seasonal strength, autocorrelation, linearity, aspects forecastability. See online Forecasting: Principles Practice textbook detailed description features. Rather inspecting dozens features one time, often useful summarise smaller feature space.
code: tourism_features <- tourism_gts |>   features(Trips, feature_set(pkgs = "feasts")) tourism_features #> # A tibble: 45 × 50 #>    State           Purpose      trend_strength seasonal_strength_year #>    <chr*>          <chr*>                <dbl>                  <dbl> #>  1 ACT             Business              0.526                  0.405 #>  2 ACT             Holiday               0.603                  0.453 #>  3 ACT             Other                 0.502                  0.254 #>  4 ACT             Visiting              0.600                  0.136 #>  5 ACT             <aggregated>          0.725                  0.184 #>  6 New South Wales Business              0.785                  0.635 #>  7 New South Wales Holiday               0.750                  0.891 #>  8 New South Wales Other                 0.874                  0.303 #>  9 New South Wales Visiting              0.831                  0.733 #> 10 New South Wales <aggregated>          0.908                  0.760 #> # ℹ 35 more rows #> # ℹ 46 more variables: seasonal_peak_year <dbl>, seasonal_trough_year <dbl>, #> #   spikiness <dbl>, linearity <dbl>, curvature <dbl>, stl_e_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   stl_e_acf10 <dbl>, acf1 <dbl>, acf10 <dbl>, diff1_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   diff1_acf10 <dbl>, diff2_acf1 <dbl>, diff2_acf10 <dbl>, season_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   pacf5 <dbl>, diff1_pacf5 <dbl>, diff2_pacf5 <dbl>, season_pacf <dbl>, #> #   zero_run_mean <dbl>, nonzero_squared_cv <dbl>, zero_start_prop <dbl>, …

what: Reducing the feature space with PCA
title: icomb
text: use principal component analysis (PCA) obtain low-dimensional representation features. coordinates .fittedPC1 .fittedPC2 summarize dominant variation across extracted series features.
code: pcs <- tourism_features |>   select(-State, -Purpose, -zero_run_mean, -zero_start_prop, -zero_end_prop) |>   prcomp(scale = TRUE) |>   broom::augment(tourism_features) pcs #> # A tibble: 45 × 96 #>    .rownames State           Purpose      trend_strength seasonal_strength_year #>    <chr>     <chr*>          <chr*>                <dbl>                  <dbl> #>  1 1         ACT             Business              0.526                  0.405 #>  2 2         ACT             Holiday               0.603                  0.453 #>  3 3         ACT             Other                 0.502                  0.254 #>  4 4         ACT             Visiting              0.600                  0.136 #>  5 5         ACT             <aggregated>          0.725                  0.184 #>  6 6         New South Wales Business              0.785                  0.635 #>  7 7         New South Wales Holiday               0.750                  0.891 #>  8 8         New South Wales Other                 0.874                  0.303 #>  9 9         New South Wales Visiting              0.831                  0.733 #> 10 10        New South Wales <aggregated>          0.908                  0.760 #> # ℹ 35 more rows #> # ℹ 91 more variables: seasonal_peak_year <dbl>, seasonal_trough_year <dbl>, #> #   spikiness <dbl>, linearity <dbl>, curvature <dbl>, stl_e_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   stl_e_acf10 <dbl>, acf1 <dbl>, acf10 <dbl>, diff1_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   diff1_acf10 <dbl>, diff2_acf1 <dbl>, diff2_acf10 <dbl>, season_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   pacf5 <dbl>, diff1_pacf5 <dbl>, diff2_pacf5 <dbl>, season_pacf <dbl>, #> #   zero_run_mean <dbl>, nonzero_squared_cv <dbl>, zero_start_prop <dbl>, …

what: Joining features, node inclusion, and accuracy
title: icomb
text: compare statistical features reconciliation behaviour, join together PCA representation node inclusion indicator icomb, base-model accuracy measures gives single data frame linking series features, inclusion status, forecast accuracy metrics.
code: all_info <- glance_output |>   filter(.model == "icomb") |>   select(State, Purpose, .included) |>   full_join(pcs, by = c("State", "Purpose"))  all_info <- accuracy(fit) |>   select(-.model, -.type) |>   full_join(all_info, by = c("State", "Purpose")) all_info #> # A tibble: 45 × 105 #>    State        Purpose       ME  RMSE   MAE      MPE  MAPE  MASE RMSSE     ACF1 #>    <chr*>       <chr*>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 ACT        … Business    4.90  32.7  26.7  -1.52   18.6  0.703 0.739  0.0312  #>  2 ACT        … Holiday     3.63  38.9  28.5  -2.00   18.6  0.839 0.842  0.110   #>  3 ACT        … Other       1.32  12.4  10.2 -16.0    42.6  0.789 0.773  0.0614  #>  4 ACT        … Visiting    3.28  31.1  23.2  -0.622  12.2  0.684 0.704  0.0737  #>  5 ACT        … <aggregat…  7.91  60.9  49.0   0.0964  9.75 0.709 0.697  0.0913  #>  6 New South W… Business    9.69 128.  102.   -0.0675  8.10 0.785 0.789 -0.0967  #>  7 New South W… Holiday     8.36 168.  136.   -0.0168  4.56 0.805 0.785  0.00534 #>  8 New South W… Other       5.91  41.4  34.9   0.0551 12.1  0.851 0.828 -0.0737  #>  9 New South W… Visiting   13.9  151.  124.    0.202   5.21 0.696 0.699  0.0391  #> 10 New South W… <aggregat… 32.6  300.  243.    0.268   3.53 0.731 0.725 -0.0306  #> # ℹ 35 more rows #> # ℹ 95 more variables: .included <lgl>, .rownames <chr>, trend_strength <dbl>, #> #   seasonal_strength_year <dbl>, seasonal_peak_year <dbl>, #> #   seasonal_trough_year <dbl>, spikiness <dbl>, linearity <dbl>, #> #   curvature <dbl>, stl_e_acf1 <dbl>, stl_e_acf10 <dbl>, acf1 <dbl>, #> #   acf10 <dbl>, diff1_acf1 <dbl>, diff1_acf10 <dbl>, diff2_acf1 <dbl>, #> #   diff2_acf10 <dbl>, season_acf1 <dbl>, pacf5 <dbl>, diff1_pacf5 <dbl>, …

what: Visualizing series inclusion in feature space
title: icomb
text: useful first question whether included excluded series occupy different regions feature space.  plot provides compact view whether series inclusion icomb associated broad statistical profile series. interactive exploration HTML output, plot can also converted plotly.
code:
tourism_viz <- all_info |> ggplot(aes( x = .fittedPC1, y = .fittedPC2, colour = .included, text = paste0( "PC1: ", round(.fittedPC1, 2), "
", "PC2: ", round(.fittedPC2, 2), "
", "State: ", State, "
", "Purpose: ", Purpose, "
", "MAPE: ", round(MAPE, 2), "
", "RMSSE: ", round(RMSSE, 2), "
", "MASE: ", round(MASE, 2) ) )) + geom_point() tourism_viz tourism_viz |> ggplotly(tooltip = "text")

title: icomb
text: PCA useful summarizing many features , often easier interpret individual features directly. Two especially common summaries trend strength annual seasonal strength.  plot helps assess whether included series tend strongly trended, seasonal, broadly similar excluded series. interactive version can also produced
code:
tourism_feature_plot <- all_info |> ggplot(aes( x = trend_strength, y = seasonal_strength_year, colour = .included, text = paste0( "Trend_strength: ", round(trend_strength, 2), "
", "Seasonal_strength_year: ", round(seasonal_strength_year, 2), "
", "State: ", State, "
", "Purpose: ", Purpose, "
" ) )) + geom_point() tourism_feature_plot tourism_feature_plot |> ggplotly(tooltip = "text")

title: icomb
text: relationship structure inclusion series may differ travel purpose. explore , facet trend-seasonality display Purpose.  interactive version can also produced desired.
code:
tourism_purpose_plot <- all_info |> ggplot(aes( x = trend_strength, y = seasonal_strength_year, colour = .included, text = paste0( "State: ", State, "
", "Purpose: ", Purpose, "
") )) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(vars(Purpose)) tourism_purpose_plot tourism_purpose_plot |> ggplotly(text = "text")

title: icomb
text: workflow makes possible ask questions : included series tend regular forecastable? highly seasonal series likely selected icomb? inclusion associated stronger weaker base forecast accuracy? patterns differ systematically across purpose groups? answers depend data implementation details icomb, framework provides practical way connect reconciliation output interpretable series characteristics.

title: icomb
text: reconciliation methods discussed limited simple summation constraints. general, MinT based approaches information combination methods can accommodate linear constraints across system time series. present, min_trace() implementation fabletools restricted summing constraints. icomb() approach, however, flexible can used settings aggregation structure defined differently.

title: icomb
text: illustration, suppose structure defined terms averages rather totals. can construct grouped structure aggregating using mean() instead sum(). fit reconcile forecasts usual way. verify coherence alternative structure, can recompute averages bottom-level forecasts compare reconciled values. range differences close zero.
code: tourism_avg_gts <- tourism_gts |>   filter(!is_aggregated(State), !is_aggregated(Purpose)) |>   aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 Trips = mean(Trips)) fc <- tourism_avg_gts |>   model(base = ETS(Trips)) |>   reconcile(icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 55)) |>   forecast() fc |>   filter(!is_aggregated(State), !is_aggregated(Purpose), .model == "icomb") |>   aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 mean_fc = mean(.mean)) |>   full_join(fc |> filter(.model == "icomb")) |>   mutate(diff = mean_fc - .mean) |>   pull(diff) |>   range() #> Joining with `by = join_by(Quarter, State, Purpose)` #> [1] -6.821210e-13  9.094947e-13

title: icomb
text: current implementation aggregate_key() fabletools yet support arbitrary weights, limits direct construction general linear constraints. expected addressed future releases. meantime, fully general linear constraints, practical approach manually construct tsibble containing series desired structure pass directly model(). provides complete flexibility defining relationships among series.

what: Forecasting Hierarchical Time Series Using Information Combination
title: Forecasting Hierarchical Time Series Using Information Combination
text: R package icomb provides tools implementing information combination approach forecasting hierarchical time series proposed Nguyen, Vahid Wickramasuriya (2025). offers tools construct combine forecasts based different information sets, enabling improved forecast accuracy within hierarchical grouped time series structures.

title: Forecasting Hierarchical Time Series Using Information Combination
text: can install stable version CRAN: can install development version GitHub
code: install.packages("icomb") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ShanikaLW/icomb")

title: Forecasting Hierarchical Time Series Using Information Combination
text: can compute probabilistic forecasts  workflow can parallelized improve performance using future package. specifying parallelization plan via future::plan() (e.g., multisession multicore), users can control computations distributed across available workers. allows cross-validation procedure information combination approach run parallel without modifying core code, remaining flexible different computing environments. plan set, default sequential strategy used, meaning computations performed one another parallelization.
code: library(fable) library(fabletools) library(tsibble) library(dplyr) library(lubridate) library(icomb) library(ggtime)  tourism_hts <- tourism |>     aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 Trips = sum(Trips))   fit <- tourism_hts |>     model(base = ETS(Trips)) |>     reconcile(ols = min_trace(base, method = "ols"),             icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 75)) fit #> # A mable: 45 x 5 #> # Key:     State, Purpose [45] #>    State           Purpose              base ols          icomb        #>    <chr*>          <chr*>            <model> <model>      <mdl_cmb_>   #>  1 ACT             Business     <ETS(M,N,M)> <ETS(M,N,M)> <ETS(M,N,M)> #>  2 ACT             Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  3 ACT             Other        <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  4 ACT             Visiting     <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> <ETS(M,N,N)> #>  5 ACT             <aggregated> <ETS(M,A,N)> <ETS(M,A,N)> <ETS(M,A,N)> #>  6 New South Wales Business     <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  7 New South Wales Holiday      <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> <ETS(M,N,A)> #>  8 New South Wales Other        <ETS(A,N,N)> <ETS(A,N,N)> <ETS(A,N,N)> #>  9 New South Wales Visiting     <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> #> 10 New South Wales <aggregated> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> <ETS(A,N,A)> #> # ℹ 35 more rows  fit |>    forecast(h = "3 years") |>     filter(Purpose == "Holiday", State == "Victoria") |>     autoplot(filter(tourism_hts, Purpose == "Holiday",                    State == "Victoria", year(Quarter) > 2010), level = NULL) fit |>    forecast(h = "3 years", bootstrap = TRUE, times = 1000) |>    filter(Purpose == "Holiday", State == "Victoria") |>   autoplot(filter(tourism_hts, Purpose == "Holiday",                   State == "Victoria", year(Quarter) > 2010)) library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 2)  tourism_hts |>     model(base = ETS(Trips)) |>     reconcile(ols = min_trace(base, method = "ols"),             icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 75))  |>     forecast(h = "3 years")  #> # A fable: 1,080 x 6 [1Q] #> # Key:     State, Purpose, .model [90] #>    State  Purpose  .model Quarter #>    <chr*> <chr*>   <chr>    <qtr> #>  1 ACT    Business base   2018 Q1 #>  2 ACT    Business base   2018 Q2 #>  3 ACT    Business base   2018 Q3 #>  4 ACT    Business base   2018 Q4 #>  5 ACT    Business base   2019 Q1 #>  6 ACT    Business base   2019 Q2 #>  7 ACT    Business base   2019 Q3 #>  8 ACT    Business base   2019 Q4 #>  9 ACT    Business base   2020 Q1 #> 10 ACT    Business base   2020 Q2 #> # ℹ 1,070 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: Trips <dist>, .mean <dbl> plan(sequential)

what: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
text: forecast function allows generate coherent future predictions hierarchical grouped time series based fitted models.

title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
code: # S3 method for class 'mdl_icomb_lst' forecast(   object,   new_data = NULL,   h = NULL,   point_forecast = list(.mean = mean),   ... )

title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
text: object time series models used produce forecasts. new_data tsibble containing future information used forecast. h forecast horizon (can used instead new_data regular time series exogenous regressors). point_forecast point forecast measure(s) returned resulting fable. Specify named list functions accept distribution return vector. ... Additional arguments forecast model methods.

title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
text: fable containing following columns: .model: name model used obtain forecast. Taken column names models provided mable. forecast distribution. name column dependent variable model(s). multiple dependent variables exist, named .distribution. Point forecasts computed distribution using functions point_forecast argument. bootstrap = TRUE, point forecasts generated using bootstrapped sample paths.

title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
text: forecasts returned contain point forecasts distribution. specific forecast interval can extracted distribution using distributional::hilo() function. intervals stored single column using hilo class, extract numerical upper lower bounds can use fabletools::unpack_hilo().

title: Produce coherent forecasts using the information combination method — forecast.mdl_icomb_lst
code: # \donttest{ library(fable) #> Loading required package: fabletools library(fabletools) library(tsibble) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘tsibble’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, union library(distributional)  tourism_gts <- tourism |>   aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 Trips = sum(Trips))  fit <- tourism_gts |>   model(base = ETS(Trips)) |>   reconcile(ols = min_trace(base, method = "ols"),             icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 75)) fit |>   forecast(bootstrap = TRUE, times = 1000) |>   hilo(level = c(80, 95)) #> # A tsibble: 1,080 x 8 [1Q] #> # Key:       State, Purpose, .model [135] #> Loading required namespace: crayon #>    State  Purpose  .model Quarter        Trips .mean #>    <chr*> <chr*>   <chr>    <qtr>       <dist> <dbl> #>  1 ACT    Business base   2018 Q1 sample[1000]  145. #>  2 ACT    Business base   2018 Q2 sample[1000]  203. #>  3 ACT    Business base   2018 Q3 sample[1000]  197. #>  4 ACT    Business base   2018 Q4 sample[1000]  191. #>  5 ACT    Business base   2019 Q1 sample[1000]  142. #>  6 ACT    Business base   2019 Q2 sample[1000]  204. #>  7 ACT    Business base   2019 Q3 sample[1000]  197. #>  8 ACT    Business base   2019 Q4 sample[1000]  189. #>  9 ACT    Business ols    2018 Q1 sample[1000]  173. #> 10 ACT    Business ols    2018 Q2 sample[1000]  237. #> # ℹ 1,070 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: `80%` <hilo>, `95%` <hilo> # }

text: tibble contains output glance() model, added logical column .included indicating whether corresponding node present reconciliation process information combination method used.

title: Glance a mable which contains an information combination reconciliation method — glance.mdl_icomb_lst
code: # \donttest{ library(fable) library(fabletools) library(tsibble) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  tourism_gts <- tourism |>   aggregate_key(State * Purpose,                 Trips = sum(Trips))  fit <- tourism_gts |>   model(base = ETS(Trips)) |>   reconcile(ols = min_trace(base, method = "ols"),             icomb = icomb(base, train_size = 75))  fit |>   glance() #> # A tibble: 135 × 12 #>    State  Purpose  .model sigma2 log_lik   AIC  AICc   BIC   MSE  AMSE #>    <chr*> <chr*>   <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 ACT    Business base   0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  2 ACT    Business ols    0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  3 ACT    Business icomb  0.0540   -453.  919.  921.  936. 1069. 1071. #>  4 ACT    Holiday  base   0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  5 ACT    Holiday  ols    0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  6 ACT    Holiday  icomb  0.0680   -463.  940.  941.  956. 1509. 1538. #>  7 ACT    Other    base   0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #>  8 ACT    Other    ols    0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #>  9 ACT    Other    icomb  0.202    -376.  759.  759.  766.  154.  156. #> 10 ACT    Visiting base   0.0305   -450.  905.  905.  912.  965. 1038. #> # ℹ 125 more rows #> # ℹ 2 more variables: MAE <dbl>, .included <lgl> # }

title: Information combination reconciliation — icomb
text: Implements Information Combination (IComb) method forecast reconciliation, combining information multiple base forecasts regression-based framework can estimated using penalized regression techniques. penalty parameter estimated using rolling forecast origin cross-validation.

title: Information combination reconciliation — icomb
code: icomb(   models,   train_size,   alpha = 1,   standardize = FALSE,   standardize_response = FALSE,   intercept = TRUE,   lambda = NULL,   lambda_min_ratio = "expand",   nlambda = 100,   maxit = 1e+07,   thresh = 1e-07,   exact = TRUE )

title: Information combination reconciliation — icomb
text: models column models mable. train_size size initial training window. alpha elasticnet mixing parameter, \(0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\). penalty defined $$(1 - \alpha)/2\|B\|_2^2 + \alpha\|B\|_2$$ alpha = 1 group lasso penalty, alpha = 0 ridge penalty. standardize Logical flag x variable standardization, prior fitting model sequence. coefficients always returned original scale. Default standardize = FALSE. standardize_response allows user standardize response variables. Default standardize_response = FALSE. intercept intercepts fitted (default = TRUE) set zero (FALSE). lambda user supplied lambda sequence. Typical usage program compute lambda sequence based nlambda lambda_min_ratio. Supplying value lambda overrides . Supply decreasing sequence lambda values. lambda_min_ratio smallest value lambda, fraction lambda_max (data derived value coefficients zero). lambda_min_ratio = "expand" (default) sets ratio \(10^{-\lfloor \log_{10}(\lambda_{max})\rfloor-2}\) whereas lambda_min_ratio = "glmnet" sets ratio value used glmnet package. nobs < nvars, default 0.01, otherwise 1e-04. nlambda number lambda values. Default 100. maxit Maximum number passes data lambda values. Default \(10^7\). thresh Convergence threshold coordinate descent. inner coordinate-descent loop continues maximum change objective coefficient update less thresh times null deviance. Defaults value 1e-07. exact logical flag indicating whether use sequence lambda values (lambda_max lambda_best) fitting final model entire dataset. functions glmnet package designed efficiency computing entire regularization path (sequence lambda values) using "warm starts", often faster computing single fit. default TRUE.

text: 'global' model icomb coherent

what: Calculate a sequence of penalty parameters — lambda_path
title: Calculate a sequence of penalty parameters — lambda_path
text: Generates decreasing sequence regularization parameters penalized multivariate regression estimating maximum lambda data constructing log-spaced path minimum value, options standardization handling zero-variance predictors responses.

title: Calculate a sequence of penalty parameters — lambda_path
code: lambda_path(   x,   y,   alpha = 1,   standardize = FALSE,   standardize_response = FALSE,   intercept = TRUE,   lambda_min_ratio = "expand",   nlambda = 100 )

title: Calculate a sequence of penalty parameters — lambda_path
text: x Input matrix, dimension nobs x nvars; row observation vector. nvars greater one. words x 2 columns. x can sparse matrix format (inherit class "sparseMatrix" package Matrix). y matrix quantitative responses. alpha elasticnet mixing parameter, \(0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\). penalty defined $$(1 - \alpha)/2\|B\|_2^2 + \alpha\|B\|_2$$ alpha = 1 group lasso penalty, alpha = 0 ridge penalty. standardize Logical flag x variable standardization, prior fitting model sequence. coefficients always returned original scale. Default standardize = FALSE. standardize_response allows user standardize response variables. Default standardize_response = FALSE. intercept intercepts fitted (default = TRUE) set zero (FALSE). lambda_min_ratio smallest value lambda, fraction lambda_max (data derived value coefficients zero). lambda_min_ratio = "expand" (default) sets ratio \(10^{-\lfloor \log_{10}(\lambda_{max})\rfloor-2}\) whereas lambda_min_ratio = "glmnet" sets ratio value used glmnet package. nobs < nvars, default 0.01, otherwise 1e-04. nlambda number lambda values. Default 100.

title: Calculate a sequence of penalty parameters — lambda_path
